network.overall_strategy: This network centers on the literature-supported conclusion that valine is most plausibly formed by Strecker chemistry from isobutyraldehyde, ammonia, and hydrogen cyanide, with hydrolysis of the corresponding aminonitrile as the main productive exit. To satisfy the literature, the network explicitly treats isobutyraldehyde as the principal bottleneck and includes only low-selectivity, geochemically plausible upstream sources: spark-discharge/radical production in reduced atmospheres, meteoritic delivery, and NiS-catalyzed aldol diversification from acetaldehyde-rich mixtures. A parallel, more speculative but metabolically congruent branch proceeds through alpha-ketoisovalerate and reductive amination on iron- or nickel-bearing minerals. Surface pathways supply HCN, aminonitrile stabilization, Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin chemistry, and concentration; hydrothermal pathways supply reduced carbon, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and reductive amination conditions; biochemical settings complete hydrolysis, transamination-linked interconversion, and peptide incorporation. The network is intentionally convergent and explicitly marks speculative connectors where the literature identifies unresolved gaps.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction and C-C buildup to pyruvate

conditions.other: alkaline vent-like conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich; network-compressing step representing reported nonenzymatic CO2 reduction/carboxylation sequences to alpha-keto acids

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reduction of CO2 to reduced C1/C2 species followed by further carboxylative C-C bond formation to an alpha-keto acid pool including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate and related alpha-keto acids are plausible products in hydrothermal iron-mineral systems and are central hubs in prebiotic protometabolism; this compressed representation follows the reported hydrothermal accessibility of alpha-keto acids and reductive carbon fixation logic, though the exact direct pyruvate-forming sequence remains network-level rather than single-study specific (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and Fe/Ni-type hydrothermal mineral surfaces
conditions.other: hydrothermal FTT-like regime; aldehydes are minor members of a broader reduced organic product mixture

reactions.mechanism: CO2/CO hydrogenation on iron-bearing mineral surfaces produces reduced carbonyl compounds, including acetaldehyde, within complex hydrothermal product distributions.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry is literature-supported as a source of reduced organics and aldehyde precursors rather than direct amino acid synthesis; acetaldehyde is a realistic feedstock for downstream branching chemistry (McCollom, Ritter & Simoneit, 1999).

reactions.name: Reduced-atmosphere spark/discharge production of HCN and formaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral collection and concentration on TiO2 and clay surfaces after atmospheric discharge formation
conditions.other: electric discharge/reducing atmospheric input into aqueous reservoir; minerals act as concentrative surface agents, not the primary energy source

reactions.mechanism: Spark-generated atmospheric nitriles and carbonyls dissolve into surface waters, where mineral surfaces aid retention and concentration.
reactions.reasoning: The research emphasizes Miller-Urey style discharge chemistry as a validated source of HCN and aldehydes feeding Strecker amino acid synthesis, including valine; the mineral annotation reflects obligatory surface agents and plausible concentration rather than claiming TiO2 alone generated HCN from NH3 (Miller, 1953; Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Surface hydrolysis/photochemical conversion of HCN to formamide

conditions.other: UV-exposed surface pools; formamide accumulates in drying settings

reactions.mechanism: Hydration and photochemically mediated conversion of cyanide-derived carbon-nitrogen species yields formamide in UV-active surface environments.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a recognized prebiotic solvent/intermediate in UV-active surface chemistry, and TiO2 is specifically supported as a crustal photocatalyst relevant to formamide chemistry; inclusion here supports the experimentally motivated N-formyl aminonitrile protection branch (Saladino et al., 2006; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2023).

reactions.name: NiS-catalyzed aldol diversification from acetaldehyde to branched aldehyde mixtures including isobutyraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: transition-metal sulfide surfaces representing NiS-like aldol diversification chemistry
conditions.other: low-selectivity aldol/rearrangement network in evaporitic or geothermal pools; branched aldehydes are minor products

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde undergoes mineral-mediated aldol diversification and fragmentation/reduction within a complex mixture, producing low yields of branched C4 aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The research explicitly identifies branched aldehyde generation from simpler feedstocks as the core unresolved bottleneck and points to NiS-catalyzed acetylene/acetaldehyde diversification as one of the best available, but low-selectivity, routes to higher branched aldehydes. This step is therefore included as a constrained, low-confidence connector rather than a selective transformation (Preiner et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Meteoritic delivery of isobutyraldehyde-equivalent branched carbonyl inventory to surface pools

conditions.catalyst: mineral adsorption and concentration after exogenous delivery
conditions.other: impact-derived or meteoritic organics delivered to pond or tidal-flat settings; network connector representing exogenous branched carbonyl supply

reactions.mechanism: Exogenous organic input supplies complex carbonyl mixtures that are trapped and concentrated on mineral-rich surface deposits, increasing local branched aldehyde availability.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report highlights meteoritic delivery as a realistic source of complex organic mixtures relevant to amino acid synthesis and notes widespread abiotic valine occurrence in carbonaceous meteorites. This reaction is a network-level import step, not a claim that HCN chemically converts into isobutyraldehyde; HCN is included as a co-delivered/associated surface feedstock hub to preserve connectedness of the bipartite graph while maintaining the exogenous-source interpretation (Kvenvolden et al., 1970; Cronin & Pizzarello, 1983; Pizzarello & Cronin, 2000).

reactions.name: Strecker formation of valine aminonitrile from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces in aqueous alkaline pools
conditions.other: pH about 9 to maximize free NH3; reversible imine formation followed by cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine/Schiff base, followed by nucleophilic cyanide addition to yield the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of valine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best experimentally validated route to valine and directly follows the literature: isobutyraldehyde is the specific Strecker aldehyde precursor for valine, aminonitrile formation is central, and mildly alkaline aqueous conditions are favored (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Keefe, Newton & Miller, 1995; Devergne, Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta, 2021).

reactions.name: Formamide-mediated N-formylation of valine aminonitrile

conditions.other: drying or low-water formamide medium; protective derivatization against Strecker reversal

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile nitrogen is formylated in formamide-rich media, generating a more persistent protected aminonitrile reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: N-formylation of aminonitriles in formamide has been shown to stabilize Strecker intermediates against retrogression and is therefore a literature-grounded branch that improves persistence of valine precursors in surface settings (Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis through amide intermediate; irreversible step drives overall Strecker sequence

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis through an amide intermediate yields racemic valine and constitutes the committed productive exit from Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of the aminonitrile is the irreversible, product-forming step in Strecker valine synthesis, with aminonitrile lifetimes and hydrolysis behavior specifically discussed in the literature (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Devergne et al., 2021; Miller, 1998).

reactions.name: Deprotection and hydrolysis of N-formyl valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: rehydration after dry formamide phase; hydrolysis removes formyl protection and nitrile functionality

reactions.mechanism: Upon return to aqueous conditions, the protected aminonitrile is hydrolyzed and deformylated to release valine.
reactions.reasoning: This step extends the experimentally motivated aminonitrile protection logic into the amino acid product pool; while less directly demonstrated specifically for valine than unprotected aminonitriles, it is a necessary literature-consistent connector from the Green and Sutherland protection regime to amino acid release (Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin access to valine hydantoin from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type conditions with cyanide, ammonia, and dissolved CO2/carbonate

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl compound, cyanide, ammonia, and carbon dioxide/carbonate interact to generate a hydantoin intermediate that bridges Strecker-type and alpha-keto-acid-related chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The research specifically identifies the Bucherer-Bergs route as a literature-supported variant that connects aldehyde-based Strecker chemistry with hydantoin intermediates in the presence of CO2, HCN, and NH3 (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen & Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine hydantoin to valine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin releases valine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin intermediates are established Bucherer-Bergs precursors to amino acids and provide an alternative product reservoir converging on valine (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoisovalerate from pyruvate-rich carbonyl chemistry

conditions.other: nonenzymatic carbonyl condensation network; included as a speculative but metabolically congruent connector and acknowledged bottleneck

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate participates in nonenzymatic C-C bond-forming chemistry with acetaldehyde-derived carbonyl species, yielding branched alpha-keto acid products including alpha-ketoisovalerate through a compressed acetolactate-like network.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies alpha-ketoisovalerate as an alternative entry point to valine and notes its possible formation through pyruvate plus acetaldehyde condensation or broader nonenzymatic Krebs-like chemistry, but also emphasizes that direct prebiotic experimental demonstration remains lacking. This reaction is therefore explicitly speculative and used only because it is necessary to connect the literature-supported reductive amination branch (Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Copley, Smith & Morowitz, 2005; Krishnamurthy group, 2025 PMC; report notes unresolved status).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S-pyrite-forming or freshly precipitated FeS; analogous extension to Ni-catalyzed H2 systems at pH 8
conditions.other: alkaline to near-neutral conditions; vent or vent-fringe mineral system; substrate-specific extension to alpha-ketoisovalerate is plausible but incompletely validated

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reductive amination converts the alpha-keto acid to the corresponding amino acid using ammonia and reducing equivalents from FeS/H2S or H2/mineral systems.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is a literature-supported prebiotic route, though specific data for alpha-ketoisovalerate are more inferential than for pyruvate. The chemistry is nonetheless metabolically congruent and directly relevant to valine's biosynthetic analog pathway (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide formation from valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: repeated wet-dry cycling in evaporitic pools; stoichiometrically represents self-condensation of two valine molecules under ester-mediated dehydration regimes

reactions.mechanism: Concentration and dehydration during wet-dry cycling drive peptide bond formation from valine monomers to short oligopeptides.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycling is a well-supported route for prebiotic peptide bond formation and provides downstream biochemical relevance for valine once formed; this node represents incorporation into peptides rather than a route required for valine synthesis itself (Forsythe et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker via discharge-derived HCN and aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced carbon supplies acetaldehyde; surface discharge chemistry supplies HCN; low-selectivity aldol diversification yields isobutyraldehyde; Strecker aminonitrile hydrolysis furnishes valine.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker with exogenous branched aldehyde input
pathways.description: HCN is produced in surface atmospheric/discharge settings while branched aldehydes are supplied exogenously and concentrated on minerals before Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Protected aminonitrile route from aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: After Strecker formation, valine aminonitrile is trapped as an N-formyl derivative in formamide-rich drying pools and later hydrolyzed to valine.

pathways.name: Protected aminonitrile route with exogenous branched aldehyde source
pathways.description: Exogenous branched carbonyl delivery bypasses the weakest endogenous branching step; aminonitrile stabilization in formamide protects the Strecker intermediate before hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route from aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: Aldol diversification produces a branched aldehyde that enters Bucherer-Bergs chemistry with HCN, NH3, and CO2 to form valine hydantoin, then hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs/hydantoin route with exogenous branched aldehyde source
pathways.description: Mineral-concentrated exogenous branched carbonyls enter Bucherer-Bergs chemistry and converge on valine through a hydantoin intermediate.

pathways.name: Alpha-keto acid reductive amination branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate and acetaldehyde feed a speculative but metabolically congruent alpha-ketoisovalerate node, which is reductively aminated on FeS/pyrite-type minerals to valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface mixed Strecker route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal FTT-like chemistry supplies acetaldehyde while surface discharge chemistry provides HCN; the two streams intersect in pond chemistry to yield valine through the canonical aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate branch feeding biochemical valine pool and peptide incorporation
pathways.description: A hydrothermal protometabolic alpha-keto acid route yields valine, which is then incorporated into simple peptides during wet-dry cycling.

pathways.name: Surface Strecker branch feeding biochemical peptide incorporation
pathways.description: The best-supported Strecker sequence produces valine, which then enters peptide-forming cycles in concentrated prebiotic pools.
